Q1: Our approaches (for any applications) are primarily focused on individual counseling style conversations. Either of the mentioned modalities if can be provided through a typical CBT methodology should be covered, for example substance use counseling and that too for more than one diversity is relevant. Considering dataset size constraints, cultural adaption is a gap, we agree, and that can be mitigated as we expand on datasets with more representation.
Q2: As mentioned earlier these are communicative labels that capture the intent and flow of dialogue and they are generally enough to fit into most conversation settings regardless of the domain. The attributes the question highlights around community specific moves and potential annotator bias are genuinely important observations but they sit more in the space of complementary work rather than affecting current label set. The dialogue act taxonomy is not claiming to capture cultural nuance and it does not need to in order to be useful for guiding chatbot dialogue trajectory.
Q3: In practice utterances in counseling conversations can carry more than one communicative intent at the same time and this is something we have noticed in our own work on dialogue systems. So yes there are cases where a single label feels like an oversimplification. For downstream chatbot systems this matters because if classification feeds into a response generation pipeline a multi-label or ranked output could lead to more contextually appropriate responses. The benchmark as a starting point for single label evaluation is still useful and we would treat multi-label considerations as an extension.
